does: This paper introduces CircuitLasso, a sparse linear regression-based method for learning sparse circuits in large language models that scale to high-dimensional sparse autoencoder (SAE) features. The core contribution is replacing computationally expensive intervention-based circuit discovery with efficient linear regression that directly identifies which SAE features across model layers causally influence target predictions. CircuitLasso recovers circuits with structural accuracy matching state-of-the-art intervention methods while requiring a fraction of the computational cost, and demonstrates its utility by uncovering how interpretable semantic features propagate through models and by leveraging discovered circuits to improve domain-generalization performance with reduced computational overhead.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability agenda of learning sparse circuits to explain LLM behavior, extending recent advances in using sparse autoencoders to decompose polysemantic neurons into interpretable features. The paper responds to a key scalability bottleneck in existing circuit learning: intervention-based approaches like those using edge attribution patch methods are computationally prohibitive when applied to high-dimensional SAE feature spaces. CircuitLasso draws on classical sparse regression techniques, adapting them to the circuit discovery problem and combining insights from neuron-level circuit work with feature-level interpretability, building a bridge between mechanistic interpretability and practical scalability challenges identified in prior work.
enables: By making circuit learning computationally tractable at scale, this work enables broader application of mechanistic interpretability to understanding real model behaviors across layers and time steps. The efficient recovery of feature-level circuits opens pathways for using interpretability insights in downstream applications, as demonstrated by the domain-generalization results. Future work can leverage CircuitLasso to systematically map how semantic information flows through large models, potentially informing model editing, adversarial robustness, and controllability research. The approach also suggests that sparse linear regression may unlock other interpretability problems previously constrained by computational budgets, expanding what we can efficiently discover about model internals.
